markdown:
## There Are 4 Phases of Mitosis


<definition term="Mitosis">Mitosis is a type of **cell division** in which a **single** parent cell divides to produce **two** genetically identical daughter cells, each containing the same number of chromosomes as the parent cell.</definition>

1. Mitosis is divided into four distinct phases: **prophase**, **metaphase**, **anaphase**, and **telophase**.
2. Each phase plays a critical role in ensuring the **accurate distribution** of chromosomes.

<callout type="warning">* Don't assume the phases of mitosis are separate.
* The process is **continuous **and are only divided into stages for clarity.</callout>

## Prophase Prepares Cells for Division


> **Prophase** is the first phase of mitosis, where chromosomes condense, the nuclear envelope breaks down, and the mitotic spindle begins to form.

![Prophase](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/66167fe6e27b6ce00b552845c0a566de3f88b549-2408x1934.png)

1. During prophase, the **chromatin** (loosely packed DNA) **condenses** into visible **chromosomes**.
2. Each chromosome consists of two **sister chromatids** joined at a **centromere**.
3. The **nuclear envelope** begins to break down, allowing the chromosomes to move freely in the cytoplasm.
4. **Microtubules** assemble into a **mitotic spindle**, which will guide the chromosomes during division.

<callout type="tip">Remember: Prophase is all about **preparation **condensing DNA and assembling the machinery for division.</callout>

## Metaphase Aligns the Chromosomes


> **Metaphase** is the second phase of mitosis, where chromosomes align at the cell’s equator, attached to spindle fibers.

![Metaphase](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/7992f9841917aff5aa12dbf8b359784f1ac6735f-2172x1842.png)

1. Chromosomes line up along the **metaphase plate** (the cell’s equator).
2. **Spindle fibers** attach to the **kinetochores** (protein structures) on the centromeres of each chromatid.
3. This alignment ensures that each daughter cell will receive one copy of each chromosome.

<callout type="note">Don’t confuse the metaphase plate with a physical structure, it’s an **imaginary line**where the chromosomes align.</callout>

## Anaphase Separates the Sister Chromatids


> **Anaphase** is the third phase of mitosis, where sister chromatids are pulled apart to opposite poles of the cell.

![Anaphase](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/062b21d9f0b9fd742e4596cef417a7f583545499-2264x1918.png)

1. The **cohesin proteins** holding the sister chromatids together are **broken down**.
2. **Spindle fibers** shorten, pulling the **sister chromatids** apart toward opposite poles of the cell.
3. Once separated, each chromatid is considered an **individual chromosome**.

<callout type="tip">Think of anaphase as the **“apart”**phase, chromatids are pulled away from each other.</callout>

## Telophase Reforms and Relaxes the Cell


> **Telophase** is the final phase of mitosis, where nuclear envelopes reform around the separated chromosomes, which decondense back into chromatin.

![Telophase](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/32a60645a300f43f3f5f01f6f1a12c492111afd0-2340x1898.png)

1. **Nuclear envelopes** reform around the two sets of chromosomes at each pole.
2. The chromosomes **decondense** back into **chromatin**, becoming less visible under a microscope.
3. The **spindle fibers** disassemble, and the cell prepares for **cytokinesis** (the division of the cytoplasm).

<callout type="note">By the end of telophase, the cell has **two distinct nuclei**, each with a complete set of chromosomes.</callout>

## Cytokinesis Completes Cell Division


> **Cytokinesis** is the process of dividing the cytoplasm to form two separate daughter cells.

![Cytokinesis](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/987f73fd852e652e9cbdeed304094b993e2ae18c-2070x1872.png)

1. In **animal cells**, a **cleavage furrow** forms, pinching the cell into two.
2. In **plant cells**, a **cell plate** forms, eventually developing into a new cell wall.

<callout type="self_review">* At which stage do spindle fibers attach to the centromeres of each chromatid? 
* When do spindle fibers shorten? 
* When does chromatin condense?</callout>
